Standard_4WD_Mobile_Robot_Simulation_Arc: Figure: File-level architecture of a generic 4WD mobile robot simulation. Key files and nodes include the robot’s URDF/Xacro description (green) with links to mesh assets and embedded sensor plugins (LiDAR, camera, IMU), the world SDF and environment models (green) for Gazebo, various launch files (blue) for Gazebo, robot spawn, Navigation2, teleop, and ROS-Gazebo bridging, configuration files (yellow) such as Nav2 params, map YAML/PNG, ros2_control diff-drive controller YAML, and the bridge topics YAML. Runtime ROS nodes (oval) like robot_state_publisher (publishing TF frames), the Nav2 stack (AMCL, planner, controller, etc.), teleop node, and the diff-drive controller interface with the Gazebo simulation via the ROS-Gazebo bridge (orange diamond). Arrows indicate file usages and runtime data flow (e.g. Gazebo loads the world, the spawn launch inserts the robot and starts robot_state_publisher and controllers, sensor data (LaserScan) is bridged from Gazebo to ROS, and navigation or teleop commands (cmd_vel) are sent from ROS to Gazebo. TF frame transformations (map, odom, base_link, sensor frames) are produced by Nav2 and the state publisher.
TurtleBotstyle_4WD_Robot_Simulation_Arch: Figure: File-level architecture of a TurtleBot-style 4WD robot simulation. This diagram mirrors the standard setup with TurtleBot-specific file naming. The TurtleBot’s URDF/Xacro description (green) defines the robot links and includes sensor plugins, referencing mesh assets for the base and wheels. A TurtleBot world SDF (green) defines the environment and includes necessary models. Launch files (blue) for Gazebo, spawning the TurtleBot, Nav2 bringup, teleop, and the ROS-Gazebo bridge orchestrate the simulation. Configuration files (yellow) include TurtleBot’s Navigation2 parameters, a predefined map (YAML + PNG), the diff-drive controller config, and bridge topics for ros_gz_bridge. At runtime, the TurtleBot’s robot_state_publisher broadcasts TF frames, the Nav2 stack (AMCL, planner, etc.) uses the sensor data (e.g. LiDAR scans bridged from Gazebo) and map to perform navigation, and teleop or Nav2 outputs cmd_vel commands that are bridged to Gazebo to drive the robot. The ROS-Gazebo bridge (orange) connects Ignition/Gazebo simulation topics with ROS 2 topics, enabling sensor data flow and control commands between the simulated TurtleBot and ROS 2 Nav2 stack.
